skill.name: python-sdk
skill.description: Use the inference.sh Python SDK to run AI apps, build agents, stream results, manage files, and integrate model workflows.
skill.summary: Build Python applications and agents with the inference.sh SDK.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The skill is documentation-only, and most static findings are Markdown examples for legitimate SDK use rather than code that runs during installation. Human review confirmed unsafe examples that use eval() on tool-call arguments, plus powerful agent patterns for code execution, webhooks, file access, and secret-backed API calls. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the unsafe examples should be fixed before publication.

high_findings.title: Unsafe eval() in Tool Handler Examples
high_findings.description: The tool handling examples evaluate tool-call arguments with eval(). If copied into an agent, model-controlled or user-controlled expressions could execute arbitrary Python code in the host process.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact eval() calls operate on call.args values inside tool handlers. This is a classic code execution pattern with clear agent-controlled input flow.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Agent Capabilities Require Strong User Controls
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows agents with code execution, file deletion tools, webhook calls, web search, and secret-backed API access. These are legitimate SDK capabilities, but unsafe deployments could expose local files, credentials, or external systems to agent actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are legitimate SDK patterns and include approval guidance in places. The risk remains meaningful because they combine agent autonomy with code, filesystem, network, and credentialed integrations.

medium_findings.title: File Upload and Download Examples Touch Local Paths
medium_findings.description: The file handling reference demonstrates reading local files, uploading file paths, downloading remote output, and creating temporary files. This is expected for an SDK, but users need clear boundaries for sensitive files and untrusted URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and semantically legitimate. The concern is deployment misuse, not evidence of malicious exfiltration in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but reviewed locations are Markdown fences, shell install snippets, npx skill installation examples, or Python SDK examples. No hidden command execution code was found in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are documentation examples and not executable package scripts. The front matter does allow Bash for pip and python, so command capability is still noted as a risk factor.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Secrets and URLs in Examples
low_findings.description: The API keys, Slack webhook, GitHub token, and example URLs appear to be placeholders or instructional references. No real credential or unauthorized exfiltration endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values use obvious placeholders such as inf_..., inf_your_key, and example.com. They still teach users how to connect secrets and webhooks, so they remain documented risk factors.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Code Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: Tool-call arguments are passed directly to eval() in calculator examples. Replace these examples with a safe parser or a constrained arithmetic evaluator.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous operation is direct and located in example code that users may copy into production handlers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Enabled Code Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows internal agent code execution enabled through SDK configuration. This is powerful and should require sandboxing, isolation, and explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code execution setting is intentional SDK functionality, not hidden behavior. The confidence is high for capability presence and moderate for real-world misuse risk.

content.user_title: Build Python AI Apps with inference.sh
content.value_statement: Python teams need reliable examples for running AI apps, agents, streaming, and file workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused SDK guidance for inference.sh projects.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and authenticate the inferencesh Python package.
Explains synchronous, asynchronous, streaming, and fire-and-forget app execution.
Documents file uploads, downloads, attachments, temporary files, and multipart upload patterns.
Provides agent patterns for template agents, ad-hoc agents, sub-agents, and human approval.
Covers tool builder types for client tools, app tools, webhook tools, and internal tools.
Includes sessions guidance for warm workers and stateful execution.
limitations: It is documentation and examples, not the SDK package itself.
It assumes access to inference.sh services and a valid API key.
Some examples use placeholder app names, URLs, paths, and secrets.
Several advanced examples need security review before production use.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI App Calls
use_cases.description: Use the examples to install the SDK, authenticate, run apps, stream status, and handle results.

use_cases.title: Build Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Create template agents, ad-hoc agents, tools, sub-agent delegation, and approval flows.

use_cases.title: Automate File-Based Pipelines
use_cases.description: Upload inputs, attach files to agent messages, download generated outputs, and process batches asynchronously.

prompt_templates.title: Run My First App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to show the shortest safe path to install inferencesh, authenticate with an environment variable, and run one inference.sh app.

prompt_templates.title: Add Streaming Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to adapt my synchronous inference.sh call so it streams status updates and handles completed or failed tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Agent with Approval
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to design a Python agent with one client tool, one app tool, and human approval for sensitive actions.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production SDK Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to review my inference.sh integration for async limits, file handling, secret management, retries, sessions, and unsafe tool execution patterns.

output_examples.input: I need to run an image app from Python and stream progress.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with installation, environment-based authentication, streaming run flow, status handling, and error handling notes.

output_examples.input: I want an agent that can generate images and ask before sending webhooks.
output_examples.output: An agent design that separates app tools from webhook tools, requires approval for external calls, and explains where secrets should be configured.

output_examples.input: Audit my file upload workflow before production.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering local path scope, file size limits, temporary file cleanup, untrusted result URLs, and safe download handling.

best_practices: Use environment variables or managed secrets instead of hardcoded API keys.
Require human approval before tools delete files, send webhooks, or call external services.
Set concurrency limits, timeouts, retries, and cleanup paths for async and file workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not use eval() on tool-call arguments or user-provided expressions.
Do not upload arbitrary local paths without checking sensitivity and user intent.
Do not enable code execution or webhooks for agents without sandboxing and audit logging.

faq.question: What package does this skill document?
faq.answer: It documents the inferencesh Python package for inference.sh app and agent workflows.

faq.question: Does this skill run code by itself?
faq.answer: No. The package contains Markdown guidance and examples, but the examples can enable powerful SDK behavior.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What security issue should be fixed first?
faq.answer: Replace eval() calculator examples with a constrained parser or a safe arithmetic evaluator.

faq.question: Does it cover async workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async clients, parallel requests, streaming, timeouts, retries, and batch patterns.

faq.question: Does it support file workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers file paths, bytes, data URIs, downloads, attachments, multipart uploads, and temporary files.
